steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Wade argues that three pieces in Heege's manuscript came from a minstrel working in Heege's local area. His existing evidence (addressing audiences, simple rhyming, requests for payment) proves the pieces were meant for live performance — but not that the minstrel was local. A supporting statement must close that gap by establishing a regional tie.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'The three pieces contain references that presume the audience is familiar with Brackonwet and other small villages near where Heege lived.': References to specific nearby villages show the pieces were crafted for a local audience in Heege's region — directly supporting the claim that they came from a local minstrel's repertoire. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Features like simple rhyming also occur in other texts... not known to have been performed for live audiences.': This weakens Wade's argument by suggesting the performance features aren't reliable indicators. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Heege's collection contains the earliest examples, but each piece occurs in later manuscript collections.': Wide circulation suggests the pieces weren't tied to a local minstrel — this undermines the regional origin claim. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Traveling minstrels performed mainly for elite audiences, and other parts of Heege's collection reflect his interest in popular appeal.': This addresses Heege's tastes, not the geographic origin of the minstrel. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
